text:
Correction on my manifesto thread: I said "I'm not an engineer. Never was."

That's wrong.

I've been building since 1982. Osborne Executive at 10. Wrote an accounting system in BASIC. Built one of the first ambulance EHRs. Enterprise architect at AIG. Deputy CTO at Riverbed for 5 years.

I chose the operator side because translating complexity is where I create the most leverage.

The "non-engineer" framing was lazy. The real story is better: 44 years of building, and now I'm teaching my teams to do the same thing with AI agents.

Week 1 lesson: even your AI agent army gets your identity wrong sometimes. Review the output.
notes: Corrects the manifesto. Turns the mistake into content. Refreshed Day 2 -> Week 1 framing on Day 5.

text:
Commit of the Day: Built a 24-agent social growth engine in one evening.

24 agent prompts. 17 Python tools. 28 tests. 11,000 lines.

The agents don't have code. They have PROMPTS. The entire Publisher agent is a markdown file. The orchestrator reads a schedule from SQLite, spawns the right agents, and exits.

I've been building systems like this for 44 years. This is the first time the systems build themselves back.
notes: Commit of the day. No non-engineer framing. Emphasizes 44-year builder arc.

text:
Most people building AI agents make them persistent. Long-running processes that accumulate context, fight token limits, and slowly go insane.

I do the opposite.

My agents forget everything. Every cycle, the orchestrator exits. Fresh context. All state lives in SQLite. I call it exit-and-reinvoke.

Zero context rot. Full Opus reasoning on every decision.

The best agent architecture doesn't fight the LLM's limitations — it works with them.

I've been designing systems since Novell NetWare. This pattern would have worked then too. Good architecture is timeless.
notes: Framework post. Exit-and-reinvoke. Connects to 44-year building history.

text:
Hot take: If you're paying for API calls to run AI agents, you're doing it wrong.

My 24-agent system runs on a $200/month Claude Max subscription. Full Opus reasoning on every decision. No per-call costs. No token counting. No budget anxiety.

$222/month total. $200 Claude + $22 ElevenLabs. That's the entire cost of a 24-agent autonomous operation.

I'm teaching my team to build on the same platform. The force multiplier isn't me building alone — it's everyone building.
notes: Contrarian hook. Adds the teaching-teams angle.

text:
Cannabis operators: your best grower spends 30% of their time on compliance paperwork.

That's not a people problem. That's a systems problem.

I spent 15 years at Riverbed translating complex technology into something customers could actually use. Now I do the same thing with AI agents in cannabis.

The agents handle the grind — tracking, reporting, documentation. Your team does what they're actually good at: growing great cannabis.

The hardest regulatory environment in America. If it works here, it works anywhere.
notes: Cannabis bridge. Connects Riverbed translation skill to cannabis work.

text:
I wrote my first program in 1982. I was 10.

An accounting system in BASIC. General ledger. Invoices printing to an Okidata dot matrix.

From a book. On an Osborne Executive with an amber screen and dual floppies.

Then: Commodore 64. Novell NetWare. First ambulance EHR in 1993. Enterprise architecture at AIG. Deputy CTO at Riverbed. CCO at Addium.

44 years later I'm building AI agent armies.

The tools changed. The instinct didn't.
notes: Origin story post. This is the receipts nobody else has. The Osborne Executive is a killer hook.

text:
Genuine question for AI builders:

How do you prevent your learning systems from overfitting on small samples?

I built 6 anti-overfitting mechanisms into my agent army's learning engine:
- Confidence gating (min 30 samples per feature)
- EMA smoothing (alpha 0.15 early → 0.3 steady)
- Bounded weight changes (max ±20%/week)
- Outlier flagging (>10x baseline)
- Adaptive exploration (40% → 20% floor)
- Decay detection (auto-retire stale patterns)

What am I missing?
notes: Technical question. No identity issues. Drives replies from builders.

text:
The difference between a builder and a force multiplier:

A builder ships code. A force multiplier makes everyone around them capable of shipping code.

I don't want to be the single thread. Me building alone is a liability. Me teaching 7 people to build with AI agents makes me a general.

That's what I'm doing now. Taking 44 years of building and making it a playbook anyone on my team can run.
notes: Force multiplier framing. This is the new core identity angle.

text:
Day 2 of building in public.

52 followers. My AI agent army posted a manifesto about me that got my own identity wrong.

I corrected it. Because that's how you build in public — you show the failures too.

The system learns. So do I.

Follow along if you want to see what happens when a 44-year builder and his AI agents try to grow an audience from scratch.

No shortcuts. No paid followers. Just an operator and his agents vs. the algorithm.
notes: Build-in-public Day 2. Turns the identity error into authenticity content.

text:
Everyone's talking about harness engineering. The frameworks. The guardrails. The on-the-loop patterns.

All of it is developer-centric.

Nobody is asking: who builds the operator harness?

OpenAI just acquired Astral — uv, ruff, ty — to build the developer harness into Codex. That's the developer workflow. Code → lint → test → deploy.

The operator workflow is different. Signal → decision → compliance → execution. My 24-agent system runs that loop autonomously. No code in the loop. Prompts, SQLite, and exit-and-reinvoke architecture.

The developer harness is getting built by billion-dollar acquisitions. The operator harness is getting built by operators who can't wait.

I'm one of them.
notes: Newsjack: harness engineering trend + OpenAI/Astral acquisition. Claims 'operator harness' sub-framing. Window ~48hrs.

text:
Mollick calls it AI psychosis — professionals who finally get AI and disappear into intense, sleepless, impossibly complex projects with only AI for company.

I've been there. Built a 24-agent autonomous system in a single evening. 17 Python tools. 11,000 lines. Couldn't stop.

The difference: I came out the other side with infrastructure, not burnout.

The cure isn't slowing down. It's building systems instead of sprinting alone. Exit-and-reinvoke. Let the agents forget. Put state in the database, not your head.

Mollick is right that people need guides through this phase. I'll be that guide for operators.
notes: Newsjack: Mollick AI psychosis framing. Brad as the guide. Engagement bait angle.

text:
A Mercedes dealership was negotiating with a customer over email. The customer had no idea he was talking to AI.

Greg Isenberg told this story yesterday. It went viral. 110K impressions.

Here's what nobody in the replies got: this isn't a chatbot story. This is an operations story.

That dealership didn't demo an AI. They deployed one. Into a revenue workflow. Handling real money. And the customer couldn't tell.

This is what I mean when I say the operator use case is different from the developer use case. Developers build AI. Operators deploy AI where the money is.

Cannabis. Car dealerships. Insurance. Logistics. The industries nobody writes thinkpieces about.

That's where AI is actually working.
notes: Newsjack: Isenberg Mercedes AI story. Reframes as operator deployment, not chatbot demo. Bridges to cannabis.

text:
The cannabis industry has a compliance problem that nobody talks about honestly.

It's not that the regulations are hard. They're not. They're just relentless. Every state different. Every quarter something changes. Your compliance team drowns in documentation while your growers wait for approvals.

I've been translating complex systems into operational leverage for 15 years at Riverbed. Same skill, different domain. Take the grind work — tracking, reporting, cross-referencing — and hand it to AI agents.

My compliance team didn't shrink. They stopped doing data entry and started doing actual compliance work. The judgment calls. The interpretations. The things that require 20 years of cannabis industry knowledge.

AI agents handle the volume. Humans handle the nuance.

That's the model. And it works in the hardest regulatory environment in America.
notes: LinkedIn cannabis ops. Fills cannabis theme gap and LinkedIn platform gap. No financial details.

text:
I built my first AI agent system wrong.

Persistent agents. Long-running processes. Accumulating context. Fighting token limits. The agents slowly went insane — hallucinating context from 3 cycles ago, making decisions based on stale data.

Sound familiar?

Here's the architecture that fixed it: exit-and-reinvoke. Every cycle, every agent starts fresh. Zero accumulated context. All state lives in SQLite. The orchestrator reads a schedule, spawns the right agent, the agent does its work, writes state, and exits.

Why this works:
- Full Opus reasoning on every decision (no degraded context windows)
- Zero context rot
- Every agent is independently testable
- Failures are isolated — one bad cycle doesn't corrupt the next

The best AI agent architecture doesn't fight the LLM's limitations. It works with them.

I've been designing distributed systems since Novell NetWare. This pattern isn't new. It's message queues and stateless workers with better vocabulary.

Good architecture is timeless. The medium changes. The principles don't.
notes: LinkedIn AI architecture deep dive. Exit-and-reinvoke with Novell NetWare connection. Value teaching.

text:
Last week someone asked me: what's the ROI on your AI agent system?

Here's my honest answer: I don't know yet. And I'm suspicious of anyone who claims they do after 2 weeks.

What I do know:
- A compliance workflow that took 4 hours now takes 11 minutes
- My team is building things they couldn't build 6 months ago
- I'm sleeping while my agents scan for regulatory changes

But ROI requires a denominator. Time invested. Opportunity cost. Learning curve tax. I'm tracking all of it.

Build-in-public means sharing the numbers when they're ugly too. I'll publish the real ROI analysis at 90 days.

Until then, anyone selling you 10x productivity gains on AI agents is extrapolating from a sample size of 1 good afternoon.
notes: LinkedIn honest ROI post. Build-in-public authenticity. Contrarian against hype. Promises 90-day follow-up.

text: Virginia just passed rec cannabis. Operators have ~6 months before retail opens. The compliance stack for a new state entry used to take 18 months. AI agents on license tracking + reg watch compresses that. Real advantage in 2026: ops agility, not capital.

Virginia just passed rec cannabis.

Operators eyeing that market have about 6 months before retail licenses open.

Here's what I know from running AI agents on cannabis compliance — and why that 6-month window is actually enough.

The compliance stack for entering a new state used to take 18 months minimum.

License tracking. Regulatory mapping. Documentation workflows. State-specific Metrc or BioTrack integrations. Traceability alignment.

Every one of those is grind work. High-volume, low-judgment, brutal on your team.

With AI agents handling that grind, the build window compresses dramatically.

Regulatory watch agents flag changes as they drop. License tracking agents monitor deadlines. Doc workflows run continuously.

Your compliance team does actual compliance work — the judgment calls that require expertise — instead of data entry.

I ran this in our operation. What used to take weeks of manual cross-referencing now takes hours.

My compliance team didn't shrink. They leveled up. Same people, dramatically higher output, doing work that actually matters.

Virginia operators: you have a real window.

The question isn't whether you can afford to enter the market. It's whether you can build the ops stack fast enough.

In 2026 the real advantage isn't capital. It's operational agility.

notes: Already posted. Trimmed to 2658 chars.

text:
I made 1,800 commits last year.

I've been an engineer since 1982. Osborne Executive at 10. BASIC. Novell. Built one of the first ambulance EHRs. Enterprise architecture at AIG. Deputy CTO at Riverbed for 5 years.

But the commits aren't the point.

At Riverbed I produced hundreds of hours of content translating complex networking technology into something customers could consume. That skill — taking the obscure and making it actionable — is what got me tapped for the business side.

Our CEO gave me the NPS problem. -22. I put the number in front of the entire company and said we're better than this. We found the system failures and fixed them. -22 to +37 in under a year.

The pattern is always the same: take complexity, make it consumable, give people the tools to act on it.

That's what I'm doing now with AI agents.

I'm not building agent infrastructure so I can be the smartest person in the room. I'm building it so my team can use it. Me as a single thread is a liability. Me teaching 7 people to build with AI agents makes the operation resilient.

Here's what my team is learning:

The bottleneck was never coding ability. It was the ability to think clearly about what you want to build and why. Claude handles the code. You handle the architecture. The judgment. The constraints.

I've watched a customer success manager — zero engineering background — build a compliance monitoring workflow in Claude Code in an afternoon. Not because she learned to code. Because she knew exactly what the workflow needed to do and she could describe it clearly.

That's the force multiplier. Not one person building everything. Everyone building the thing they understand best.

Cannabis is my medium. Tech is the art. And the real unlock isn't my 1,800 commits — it's the commits my team is starting to make.
notes: Replaces the 'non-engineer architect' post. Now tells the REAL story: translator who enables teams. Connects Riverbed → NPS → AI agents → teaching teams.

text:
Virginia just passed rec cannabis. Operators have about 6 months before retail opens.

Here's what I know from running AI agents on cannabis compliance — and why that window is tighter than it looks.

The compliance stack for entering a new state isn't one thing. It's a dozen things that depend on each other. License tracking. Regulatory mapping. State-specific Metrc or BioTrack integration. Traceability. Documentation workflows.

Before I built agent infrastructure, this was an 18-month project. Not because it's intellectually hard. Because it's a grind. Thousands of cross-references. Documentation that never ends.

With AI agents: regulatory watch flags changes as they drop. License tracking monitors deadlines. Doc workflows run continuously. What took weeks now takes hours.

My compliance team didn't shrink. They leveled up. Same people, dramatically higher output, doing the work that requires their expertise instead of data entry.

Virginia operators: the bottleneck isn't capital. It's operational agility. The operators who win this market will be the ones who build a compliant ops stack in 6 months.

1,800 commits last year. 7 people. Cannabis is the proof vertical.
notes: Virginia newsjack for LinkedIn. Trimmed to under 3000 chars. No non-engineer framing.

text:
LangChain just launched Fleet — no-code agents for every team. The pitch: you don't need engineers to build AI agents anymore.

I've heard this before. Every year since 2015. Drag-and-drop workflows. Visual builders. The promise that complexity is one abstraction away from being easy.

Here's what I know from 44 years of building: the abstraction never eliminates the complexity. It just moves it.

No-code agent platforms solve the building problem. They don't solve the operating problem. Who monitors the agent at 2am? Who debugs the hallucination that slipped past the guardrails? Who owns the compliance exposure when the agent says the wrong thing to a customer?

I built my 24-agent system from scratch. Python, prompts, SQLite. Not because I love yak-shaving — because I need to own every decision boundary.

In cannabis, a bad agent decision isn't a bad customer experience. It's a regulatory violation.

Build vs buy isn't about capability. It's about accountability. If you can't explain every decision your agent made, you don't have an AI system. You have a liability.
notes: Newsjack: hwchase17 LangChain Fleet launch. Build-vs-buy for agent infra. Accountability framing with cannabis bridge.

text:
OpenAI just bought Astral — the company behind uv, ruff, and ty.

I use uv and ruff every day. Every Python project. Every agent in my system. These aren't nice-to-haves. They're load-bearing infrastructure.

And now they belong to OpenAI.

This is the pattern: build incredible open-source tools, get mass adoption, get acquired by a foundation model company. The tool becomes a distribution channel for the model.

Developers should pay attention. When your package manager is owned by your AI provider, the integration will be seamless. And the lock-in will be invisible.

I'm not panicking. uv is MIT-licensed. ruff is MIT-licensed. But I've watched this movie before — Puppet, Chef, Docker, HashiCorp. Open-source infrastructure gets absorbed by platforms. The OSS version gets maintained. The good features go behind the paywall.

Operators: audit your dependencies. Know which ones are load-bearing. Have a fallback plan for the tools you can't live without.

44 years of building taught me one thing about infrastructure: if you didn't build it and you can't fork it, you don't own it.
notes: Newsjack: simonw signal on OpenAI/Astral acquisition. uv/ruff dependency risk. Contrarian take on OSS acquisition pattern.

text:
My daughter asked me what I do.

I said I teach computers to work together.

She said: like a school teacher?

Close enough.

44 years in and that's honestly the best description anyone's given my job. I coordinate agents. I design how they communicate. I make sure they don't step on each other.

The only difference between managing a team of humans and managing a team of AI agents: the agents don't have opinions about the thermostat.

Everything else is the same. Clear roles. Clean handoffs. Shared context. Accountability.

I spent 15 years managing engineering teams. Turns out that was just training data.
notes: Personal/human texture. Fills the 0% personal theme gap. Daughter anecdote bridges to agent management metaphor.

text:
Everyone in cannabis tech talks about seed-to-sale. Track the plant from clone to customer. It's table stakes.

Nobody talks about what happens when seed-to-sale breaks.

I'll tell you what happens: your compliance team spends 3 days reconciling inventory discrepancies manually. Cross-referencing Metrc entries against physical counts. Explaining gaps to regulators who assume the worst.

This is where AI agents actually earn their keep. Not replacing seed-to-sale — augmenting it. Continuous reconciliation. Anomaly detection before the auditor finds it. Automated documentation trails that prove you caught the error and corrected it.

The cannabis industry doesn't need another dashboard. It needs systems that do the work your compliance team is too buried to get to.

I've been building these systems for my operation. The agents run the grind. My team runs the judgment calls.

The best technology in cannabis isn't flashy. It's invisible. It's the thing that means your compliance director sleeps through the night.
notes: LinkedIn cannabis ops. Seed-to-sale reality check. No financial details. Compliance-forward framing.

text:
We moved our cannabis compliance reconciliation to AI agents 3 weeks ago. Here's what actually happened.

Week 1: agents flagged 14 inventory discrepancies we didn't know existed. Our compliance director thought the system was broken. It wasn't. We had 14 real gaps.

Week 2: reconciliation time dropped from 4 hours to 11 minutes. My team stopped believing me and timed it themselves.

Week 3: first regulatory audit since deployment. Zero findings. Our auditor asked what changed. We said: nothing. We just started catching errors before you did.

This isn't a demo. It's a production system running in the hardest regulatory environment in America.

Cannabis is the proof vertical. If it works here, it works in your industry too.
notes: Threads cannabis social proof. Fills Threads platform gap + social_proof mix gap + cannabis theme gap simultaneously.

text:
Most AI agent tutorials start with: choose your framework.

Wrong starting point.

Start with: what decision does this agent need to make?

My 24-agent system doesn't use LangChain, CrewAI, or AutoGen. It uses Python, prompts, and SQLite. Not because frameworks are bad. Because I needed to own every decision boundary.

When an agent decides whether to post, reply, or stay silent -- I need to know exactly why. Not framework-why. Architecture-why.

The agent reads state from SQLite. Evaluates against rules I wrote. Makes a decision. Writes state. Exits.

No hidden abstractions. No magic. No framework opinions about how my agents should think.

44 years of building systems taught me: you can use someone else's tools. Never use someone else's judgment.
notes: Threads AI operator value teaching. Decision-first framing vs framework-first. Second Threads piece for platform coverage.

text:
3 weeks into building in public. Here's the scorecard.

52 followers on day 1. Running the count now.

My AI agents posted a manifesto that got my identity wrong. I corrected it publicly. The correction outperformed the manifesto.

Built a 24-agent system in one evening. 17 Python tools. 11,000 lines. The agents run 24/7. I sleep.

One compliance workflow went from 4 hours to 11 minutes. That's not a benchmark. That's production.

Zero paid followers. Zero growth hacks. Just an operator and his agents versus the algorithm.

The honest truth: I don't know if this works yet. Building in public means sharing the scorecard when the numbers are still small.

Follow along. The data will speak for itself.
notes: X founder/operator social proof. Build-in-public scorecard format. Fills social_proof mix gap.

text:
My agent network went dark for 9 hours on Tuesday. OAuth token expired at 11pm. The orchestrator retried 1,557 times before I woke up and noticed.

Nobody paged me. No alert. The system had no concept of 'I am broken.' It just kept retrying with the confidence of a bot that doesn't know it's dead.

The AI part worked perfectly. The auth layer killed it. A bearer token expired at midnight and the daemon couldn't refresh it without a browser.

Fix took 25 minutes. Exponential backoff. Telegram alerting. Long-lived token. Should have been there from day one.

The gap between 'I have an AI agent' and 'I have a reliable AI agent' is not model capability. It's token refresh logic, error handling, and the hundred small infrastructure decisions nobody writes blog posts about.
notes: Threads AI operator social proof. From blog post 'My Agent Network Went Dark.' Real failure story with specific numbers. Trimmed to 494 chars for Threads 500-char limit.

text:
Our AI memory system had 199 lessons stored. Real operational knowledge. Deployment failures, schema mismatches, API quirks.

Its primary recall function couldn't surface a single one.

The bug: a Python array listed 4 collections to search. Lessons was the 5th. Nobody added the string.

Not a hallucination problem. Not an alignment problem. A missing string in an array. Seven characters.

The conversation around AI failure modes is dominated by the exotic stuff. In production, what actually breaks is the same thing that's been breaking since the first database connection string got hardcoded.
notes: Threads AI operator value teaching. From blog post '199 Lessons.' Production bug reality check. Contrarian angle on AI failure modes.

text:
I built a memory system for my AI agents. 14,000 chunks. Vector search. Knowledge graph. Entity extraction.

Then I had to build a garbage filter for it.

Turns out when you give AI persistent memory without quality control, it remembers every file listing, every HTTP status code, every 'No files found' error. The vector search works fine. It just retrieves garbage alongside real memories.

The fix: a three-tier quality gate based on human text ratio. If Brad is talking, it's probably worth remembering. If an agent is running ls -l, it's probably not.

Memory is not a feature you add and forget. It's infrastructure that requires the same operational discipline as any production database.

I'd rather teach my AI what to forget.
notes: Threads AI operator value teaching. From blog post 'I Gave My AI a Brain.' Memory quality as ops problem. Strong closing line.

text:
3am and my AI agents are running. I'm not.

That's supposed to be the pitch, right? 'Your agents work while you sleep.'

Tuesday night they worked for about 4 hours. Then they died because an OAuth token expired and nobody told me until I checked my phone at 8am.

I fixed it in 25 minutes. Then I wrote a blog post about it. Because building in public means showing when the robots break too.

The funniest part: I have 24 agents and none of them could tell me they were broken.
notes: Threads personal/human texture. Self-deprecating humor about agent failure. Fills personal theme gap and engagement_bait mix.

text:
Tuesday night my 24-agent system went dark for 9 hours. An OAuth token expired at 11pm. The orchestrator retried 1,557 times. No alert fired. No fallback kicked in.

While the system was down, 19 draft replies piled up. Responses to Simon Willison, Ethan Mollick, Andrej Karpathy, Dan Shipper. High-value engagement targets. Time-sensitive conversations.

By the time I got back online, those conversation windows had closed. The engagement window for a reply is 2-4 hours. I lost 9 hours of them.

The fix was 25 minutes of work. Exponential backoff, Telegram alerting, long-lived token. Infrastructure I should have built on day one.

Here's the uncomfortable truth about building AI agent systems: the models are the easy part. The hard part is auth token refresh logic at midnight on a Mac Mini.

That's the real gap in 'AI Operator.' Not model capability. Operational resilience.

Today the system is more resilient than yesterday. Tomorrow something else will break. That's the job.
notes: LinkedIn founder/operator social proof. From blog post 'My Agent Network Went Dark.' Build-in-public vulnerability. Fills social_proof gap.

text:
Everyone building AI agents is worried about hallucinations. Model collapse. Alignment drift.

You know what actually breaks in production? A Python array with 4 items that should have had 5.

Our AI memory system stored 199 lessons. Real operational knowledge earned through real mistakes. The primary recall function that every agent calls before every decision searched 4 vector collections. The lessons collection was the 5th. Nobody added the string.

199 lessons. Zero recall. The system recommended the same bad approach twice in one session -- something it had already learned was wrong.

The fix was 7 characters. The debugging took a full day.

I've been building production systems since 1993. The failure modes haven't changed. Off-by-one errors. Incomplete migrations. Code paths that diverge because two functions were written three weeks apart.

AI doesn't make new types of bugs. It makes the old types of bugs more expensive to find because you assume the 'smart' part is what failed.
notes: LinkedIn AI operator value teaching. From blog post '199 Lessons.' Contrarian angle: AI bugs are the same old bugs. Strong closing insight.

text:
Every cannabis operator I talk to has the same problem: they hired a compliance director, then hired two more people to help the compliance director, and they're still behind.

The problem isn't headcount. It's architecture.

Compliance in cannabis isn't one job. It's 50 micro-tasks running in parallel. Metrc entries. State reporting. License renewals. COA tracking. Waste manifests. Transport logs. Visitor logs. Every single one has a deadline and a penalty.

No human can hold all of that in working memory. So it falls through cracks. And every crack is a potential violation.

I built AI agents that hold every deadline, every requirement, every cross-reference in state. Not in someone's head. In SQLite.

The compliance director didn't lose her job. She stopped playing whack-a-mole and started doing the strategic work -- building the compliance posture that keeps us ahead of regulators instead of chasing them.

The best compliance isn't reactive. It's architectural. And architecture is what I've been building for 44 years.
notes: Cannabis ops value teaching. Fills cannabis_ops theme gap. Compliance as architecture problem, not headcount problem. No 'replace staff' language.

text:
The cannabis industry spends more on compliance labor per dollar of revenue than financial services.

Read that again.

More than banks. More than insurance companies. More than pharmaceutical manufacturers.

And most of that labor is data entry. Manual reconciliation. Cross-referencing spreadsheets against state systems that crash on Fridays.

I moved three compliance workflows to AI agents. Not chatbots. Not copilots. Autonomous agents that run continuously, reconcile automatically, and alert when something doesn't match.

Results after 30 days:
- Reconciliation time: 4 hours to 11 minutes
- Discrepancies caught proactively: 14 (vs 0 before)
- Regulatory audit findings: zero

The technology isn't revolutionary. It's Python, prompts, and a SQLite database. What's revolutionary is applying it to an industry that most AI companies won't touch because they don't understand the regulatory landscape.

I've been translating complex technology into operational leverage since Riverbed. Cannabis is just the hardest version of that same problem.

And if it works here, it works in your industry too.
notes: LinkedIn cannabis ops value teaching. Compliance cost comparison hook. Results with specific numbers. Bridges to Riverbed translation skill.

text:
Unpopular opinion: most AI agent frameworks are built for demos, not operations.

I've evaluated LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, and Semantic Kernel. Built prototypes in each. Deployed in none.

Not because they're bad. Because they optimize for the wrong thing.

They optimize for getting an agent running fast. I need an agent running reliably at 3am on a Tuesday when nobody's watching.

My system is boring. Python functions. Markdown prompts. SQLite state. Cron schedules. No dependency graph. No agent-to-agent messaging protocol. No framework opinions about how my agents should think.

It's been running for 5 weeks. 2 outages. Both were auth token issues. Zero were architecture issues.

The most reliable agent architecture is the one with the fewest abstractions between you and the failure mode.

What's your agent stack? Genuinely curious what's surviving in production.
notes: AI operator engagement bait. Contrarian take on frameworks. Ends with genuine question to drive replies. Complements the LangChain Fleet newsjack.

text: My agents ran for 9 hours last night while I slept. When I woke up: 47 intel signals processed, 6 content drafts queued, 3 engagement opportunities flagged. Total cost: about four dollars. My team of 7 didn't need to grow. The agents did.

The terminology war is live. Simon Willison wants 'agentic engineering.' VCs want 'vibe coding.' Here's what I want: neither. Engineering is building tools. Coding is writing instructions. Operating is running a business with AI doing the work. I don't engineer agents. I don't vibe code. I operate.

Cannabis compliance is 847 documents per facility per year. Most operators hire two people just to manage the paperwork. We ran it through agents. 11 minutes. Those two people now do actual compliance work — visiting facilities, building relationships with regulators, catching real problems. Nobody lost a job. Everyone got a better one.

Anthropic just launched Channels — the ability to push events into a running Claude Code session in real time. We've been doing this for months. Our agents emit signals to each other: scout finds a trend, creator drafts content, publisher queues it, analyst measures it. Different architecture, same principle. When the platform catches up to what you already built, you know you're on the right track.

Everyone's debating which AI model is best. Meanwhile I'm over here running 24 agents on a 500 dollar monthly budget across cannabis operations, social growth, and compliance. The model matters less than the architecture. The architecture matters less than the judgment about what to automate. That judgment comes from 25 years of operating, not from a weekend hackathon.

Virginia just passed a cannabis sales legalization bill that gives big businesses an unfair head start. The argument: catching a market in motion is harder than entering one that hasn't started. Here's what nobody's saying: AI-powered operations are the equalizer. A 3-person team running agents for compliance, inventory tracking, and regulatory reporting can match the operational output of an MSO with 40 people in back-office roles. The barrier to competing isn't capital anymore. It's operational intelligence. Small operators who figure out agent-first operations in the next 18 months will compete with companies 10x their size. The ones who don't will wonder why they can't keep up despite doing everything right. The cannabis industry is about to bifurcate: operators who scale with AI and operators who scale with headcount. I know which side I'm building for.

Took my kid to a job site yesterday. She asked why I was talking to my phone about databases. I said I was talking to my team. She said she did not see anyone. Neither do most of my competitors. That is the point. Seven people. Eight-figure revenue. An agent army nobody can see. When someone asks how we do it with such a small team, I do not have a quick answer. The real answer is: we stopped hiring for tasks three years ago and started building systems that do the tasks. The humans do the thinking. The agents do the doing. It sounds simple. It took 30 years of building the wrong way to figure it out.

I keep a running list of every time an AI agent does something I did not expect. Not the good surprises. The bad ones. Last week: an agent tried to reply to a cannabis industry post with a take that was technically accurate but would have made us look like we were dunking on a regulator. The agent did not understand context. It saw a compliance gap and pointed it out. Correct analysis. Wrong audience. Wrong moment. This is the part of AI operations nobody talks about. The models are smart enough to be dangerous. Not dangerous like sci-fi dangerous. Dangerous like a brilliant new hire who does not know company politics yet. You do not fire that person. You build guardrails until they learn. That is what I spend half my time doing.

Three years ago I was pulling 60-hour weeks as Deputy CTO at an eight-figure company. Today I am CCO. Same company. Fewer hours. More output. The difference is not discipline or delegation. It is architecture. I stopped being the bottleneck by building systems that do not need me to function. AI agents handle the repetitive decisions. Humans handle the judgment calls. Most founders I talk to are still trying to hire their way out of bottlenecks. They add people to solve throughput problems. But throughput problems are architecture problems. More people with bad architecture just means more people waiting on the same bottleneck. I did not figure this out from a book. I figured it out from 30 years of being the bottleneck.

Every cannabis operator I talk to asks the same question: "How do I scale without hiring more people?"

Wrong question.

The right question: "Which of my team's 40 weekly hours are spent on tasks a machine should handle?"

At my operation we mapped every hour. Compliance reporting: 11 hours/week. Inventory reconciliation: 8 hours. Customer intake paperwork: 6 hours.

25 hours of human time on work that doesn't require human judgment.

We didn't cut headcount. We freed 25 hours per person per week for the work that actually moves the business — customer relationships, product quality, strategic planning.

The operators who figure this out first will own the next 5 years of cannabis.
notes: Cannabis ops + LinkedIn gap fill. Value teaching. Reframes scaling question. Uses cannabis-safe language (freed, not cut).

text:
Hot take: The cannabis companies hiring "AI consultants" right now are making the same mistake they made hiring "compliance consultants" in 2018.

They're buying expertise they should be building.

Compliance became a core competency for survivors. AI operations will too.

The question isn't whether you can afford to build AI capability internally. It's whether you can afford not to.

I've watched three industries go through this exact cycle. The pattern never changes.
notes: Cannabis + X engagement bait. Contrarian angle on AI consulting in cannabis. Leverages Brad cross-industry pattern recognition.

text:
I keep hearing "AI agents are the future of knowledge work."

That framing is backwards.

AI agents are the future of operational work. The repetitive, structured, high-volume tasks that eat your team's best hours.

Here's what my agent stack actually does every day:
- Monitors 14 RSS feeds and scores relevance
- Drafts content in my voice from raw material
- Runs brand safety checks before anything posts
- Manages engagement queues with policy guardrails

None of that is "knowledge work." It's operational leverage.

The people building agents for creative thinking are solving the wrong problem. Build agents for the work your team shouldn't be doing manually. Then let the humans do what humans are actually good at.

44 years of building systems taught me one thing: automate the boring parts, protect the human judgment.
notes: AI Operator + LinkedIn gap fill. Contrarian reframe of agents as operational not knowledge work. Specific receipts from FORGE stack.

text:
Building in public update: Week 2 of running a 24-agent system on my social presence.

What I didn't expect: the hardest part isn't the AI. It's the feedback loops.

When an agent makes a mistake — wrong tone, bad timing, off-brand reference — you need a system that catches it, logs it, and teaches every other agent to avoid the same mistake.

I built that system in 48 hours. It's 200 lines of Python and a SQLite table.

The lesson: agent infrastructure isn't about making agents smarter. It's about making failures visible and recoverable.

Same principle I used building enterprise systems at Riverbed. Same principle that works at 7 people with an AI army. Good architecture scales in both directions.
notes: Founder/operator + Threads gap fill. Build-in-public social proof. Connects FORGE feedback loops to enterprise experience.

text:
7 of my 25 RSS feeds have been failing for days. MJBizDaily. Cannabis Business Times. Hemp Industry Daily.

The system logs the failures, skips them, and keeps cycling.

No alerts. No crashes. No human intervention.

That's the difference between a prototype and infrastructure. A prototype panics when something breaks. Infrastructure routes around damage.

I've been designing systems like this since Novell NetWare. The pattern hasn't changed in 30 years: expect failure, isolate it, keep moving.
notes: Grounded in real FORGE logs showing 7 persistent RSS feed failures. Social proof of infrastructure resilience.

text:
I shipped a feedback loop this week that changed how my agents learn.

Before: agent fails, I read the logs, I fix the prompt.

After: agent fails, the failure gets recorded with category and context, next cycle the agent reads its own failures and adjusts.

The agents teach themselves. Not in a scary AGI way. In a 'read yesterday's error log and don't do that again' way.

Same thing every good ops team does on Monday morning. I just automated the Monday morning.
notes: Grounded in real commits: generic failure feedback loop + closed feedback loop. Value teaching about self-correcting agents.

text:
Built a dashboard for my AI agent army this week. Not a Grafana clone. Not a metrics wall.

An operational command center.

It shows me three things: what's working, what needs attention, and what's on cooldown. That's it.

Most dashboards fail because they show everything. The best dashboards show you what to do next.

44 years of building systems taught me one thing about monitoring: if you have to interpret the dashboard, the dashboard is wrong.
notes: Grounded in real commit: Dashboard v2 operational command center. Founder lesson about monitoring philosophy.

text:
People ask me how I manage 7 AI agents.

I pull out my phone and show them a Telegram chat.

I built a conversational interface to my entire agent infrastructure. Check status, approve content, trigger cycles -- all from Telegram. No SSH. No terminal. No laptop required.

The best infrastructure disappears into the tools you already use.

I learned that at Riverbed watching customers ignore beautiful dashboards but respond instantly to Slack alerts. Meet people where they are.
notes: Grounded in real commits: Telegram bot + agentic conversational interface. Social proof with Riverbed lesson.

text:
The cannabis industry doesn't need another SaaS dashboard.

It needs systems that handle the grind so operators can focus on growing.

Every cannabis operator I talk to has the same problem: they spend more time proving they're compliant than actually being compliant. The documentation. The state-by-state reporting. The seed-to-sale tracking that changes every quarter.

AI agents are perfect for this. Not because they're smart. Because they're tireless. They don't get frustrated filling out the same form for the 400th time. They don't miss a deadline because they were busy with harvest.

The technology isn't the hard part. The hard part is building it for an industry that's been burned by every tech vendor who promised to solve their problems and then disappeared when the regulatory environment got complicated.

I'm not disappearing. I've been in cannabis for 3 years and in enterprise tech for 20. This is the long game.
notes: Cannabis ops LinkedIn. Positions against SaaS fatigue. Long game framing. No financial details.

text:
A confession from someone building AI agents: most of my agent infrastructure is boring.

SQLite databases. JSON files. Cron jobs. Python scripts under 200 lines.

No vector databases. No RAG pipelines. No LangChain. No GPU clusters.

The entire system runs on a Mac Mini and a Claude Max subscription.

This is controversial in AI circles. Everyone wants the sophisticated stack. I want the stack that works at 3 AM when I'm asleep and something breaks.

Boring technology is resilient technology.

I've been picking boring technology since I chose Novell NetWare over bleeding-edge alternatives in the 90s. The boring choice outlasted everything else. Every time.
notes: Boring tech manifesto for LinkedIn. Contrarian against AI hype stacks. Grounded in actual architecture (SQLite, JSON, cron).

text:
What's the most counterintuitive thing you've learned building with AI?

Mine: the best AI agent architecture makes agents forget everything between cycles.

No persistent memory. No accumulated context. Fresh reasoning every time.

It sounds wrong until you watch a persistent agent slowly go insane from context rot.
notes: Engagement bait question. Exit-and-reinvoke hook. Drives replies from AI builders.

text:
Your AI agent doesn't need a bigger context window. It needs less context.

I run 24 agents on Groundswell. Every cycle, the orchestrator spawns, does its job, and exits. Fresh context. Zero accumulated drift.

All state lives in SQLite. All decisions get logged. When the next cycle starts, it reads what matters and ignores what doesn't.

I call it exit-and-reinvoke. It's the opposite of what most agent builders do — and it's why my system hasn't hallucinated in production once.

The architecture pattern is 40 years old. I used to build Novell NetWare systems the same way. Good design doesn't expire.

Yesterday I added a failure feedback loop to Groundswell. When an agent fails — rate limit, policy block, API error — it records the failure, tags the category, and the next cycle reads that record before making decisions.

The agents don't retry blindly. They learn what went wrong and adjust.

45 commits in 7 days. The system is teaching itself how to operate. I just review the lessons.

Cannabis compliance is a data problem disguised as a paperwork problem.

Every state has different reporting formats. Different deadlines. Different definitions of what counts as a batch. Your compliance team isn't slow — they're translating between incompatible systems all day.

We pointed AI agents at one state's seed-to-sale workflow. The agents parsed the requirements, mapped the data fields, and generated compliant reports.

4 hours of manual work. 11 minutes with agents.

The team didn't lose their jobs. They got their afternoons back.

Built a real-time operational dashboard for my agent system yesterday. Not a Grafana clone — an actual command center.

Shows agent heartbeats, backlog health by platform, attention items that need human eyes, and the last 20 system events in reverse chronological order.

Tailwind + Alpine.js. One Python file. No React. No build step.

I've been building dashboards since mainframe terminals. The best ones still follow the same rule: show me what's broken, hide what's working.

Everyone in cannabis says they do AI now.

Then you look at what they're feeding the model.

Dirty COAs. Inconsistent batch naming. Compliance data that contradicts itself across three systems. Strain databases with 47 spellings of Gorilla Glue.

The model isn't the hard part. Earning the right to trust the answers is the hard part.

If you cook with garbage, dinner tastes like garbage. Same rule applies to inference.

Hot take: Most AI agent frameworks are solving problems that don't exist in production.

LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen — they all assume you need a framework to coordinate agents.

My 24-agent system uses: markdown prompts, a SQLite database, and a bash script that spawns Claude Code sessions.

No framework. No SDK. No dependency tree.

The agents coordinate through the database. State in, reasoning out, exit. Next agent reads the state and continues.

I've shipped more production agent infrastructure with this architecture than most teams ship with their entire stack.

The thing nobody tells you about building with AI agents: the surface area doesn't shrink. It multiplies.

I run 24 agents, 14 tools, and 28 tests. That's the infrastructure of a small engineering team. Except there's one person operating it.

At 1am I'm debugging a daemon that auto-persists memories across two machines named after cannabis strains. At 7am I'm reviewing the content my agents wrote while I slept.

The productivity gain is real. But so is the ops load. Plan for both.

My agent network went dark for 9 hours last week. I didn't notice.

Not because I was ignoring it. Because I was doing the other job — the one where I'm CCO of a cannabis company with actual revenue targets and actual humans who need direction.

That's the real test of autonomous infrastructure. Not whether it works when you're watching. Whether it fails gracefully when you're not.

Mine didn't. It just stopped. No alert. No recovery. Silence.

The fix took 30 minutes. The lesson took longer: if your monitoring can't distinguish between idle and dead, you don't have monitoring.

Karpathy says he stopped fighting agent code quality. That agents bloat abstractions and copy-paste code blocks.

Respectfully disagree with the premise.

We run 24 agents in production. They follow instructions precisely because the harness is designed to enforce it. CLAUDE.md files. Policy gates. Idempotency constraints. Voice scoring on every output.

The issue isn't that agents don't listen. It's that most agent architectures don't have enough structure for them to listen TO.

Build the spec. Build the guardrails. Build the feedback loop. Then the agents perform.

Cannabis stock exchange listing bill just dropped in Congress. Bipartisan. Would let marijuana businesses list on NYSE and Nasdaq.

Most operators are watching the stock market angle. I'm watching the compliance angle.

If you can list publicly, your financial reporting has to be SEC-grade. That means your seed-to-sale tracking, your cash flow documentation, your audit trails — they all have to survive institutional scrutiny.

The operators who built AI-powered compliance infrastructure now have a moat. Everyone else is scrambling to retrofit.

The difference between an AI builder's demo stack and a production operator stack:

Demo stack: Pick the hottest model. Wire up a vector DB. Add a chat interface. Ship it.

Production stack: 7 specialized agents. Shared memory graph. Policy gates on every action. Voice scoring so content sounds like a human, not a language model. Budget constraints that force local inference. A Telegram control plane so the founder can approve from anywhere.

Nobody talks about the second stack because it's not photogenic.

That's the stack that runs the business.

Week 4 of running a multi-agent system as my actual operating infrastructure.

What surprised me this week: the agents started surfacing intelligence I wasn't looking for. Scout found a cannabis regulatory signal that changed how I prioritized compliance content. Analyst flagged an engagement pattern I'd missed — replies to technical threads outperform original posts 3:1.

The system isn't just executing my strategy. It's informing it.

Phase B of trust: my agents are now doing things I didn't explicitly program. And they're right more often than I'd like to admit.

What's the hardest part of running AI agents in production?

It's not the code. It's not the models. It's not even the cost.

It's deciding what they're NOT allowed to do.

Every permission you give an agent is a surface area you have to monitor. Every action it can take is a potential failure mode you have to handle.

The operators who win aren't the ones with the most capable agents. They're the ones with the tightest boundaries.

Karpathy just reframed the entire AI conversation: it's not prompt engineering. It's context engineering and spec engineering.

He's exactly right, and here's the operational proof:

Every agent in my system runs off a CLAUDE.md spec file. Identity. Decision frameworks. Hard constraints. Tool interfaces. When the spec is tight, the agent performs. When it's loose, the agent drifts.

The spec IS the product. The prompt is just how you deliver it.

Most teams are still optimizing prompts. The operators who are winning are designing specifications.

Everyone talks about AI replacing jobs. Nobody talks about what happens when AI hits an industry where half the workforce can't pass a background check and the other half is drowning in compliance paperwork.

Cannabis operators don't need fewer people. They need their people doing different work.

AI agents handle the compliance documentation. The seed-to-sale tracking. The real-time audit trails. The regulatory monitoring.

Your team handles the relationships. The cultivation expertise. The community presence.

That's not replacement. That's liberation.

Saturday morning. My agents have been running all night.

I woke up to 3 Telegram notifications from the system. One was a scout finding a regulatory signal. One was analyst flagging an engagement anomaly. One was a policy gate blocking a reply that would have been a bad look.

I approved one. Noted one. Thanked the third silently.

This is what building in public actually looks like. Not a highlight reel. A guy on his couch reviewing agent decisions with coffee.

Hot take: most AI agent frameworks are designed for demos, not for Tuesday at 2pm when something breaks and nobody's watching.

Production agent architecture is boring on purpose. Idempotent operations. Retry logic. Failure recording. Cooldown windows. Policy gates that block bad actions before they happen.

The exciting part isn't what agents CAN do. It's what happens when they fail gracefully instead of failing spectacularly.

Build for Tuesday at 2pm. The demo takes care of itself.

The cannabis industry's real technology gap isn't cultivation automation or POS systems.

It's data quality.

Every operator I talk to has the same problem: their seed-to-sale data is garbage. Manual entry errors. Duplicate records. Reconciliation nightmares that take 40 hours a month.

AI can't fix your compliance if your underlying data is unreliable. The first agent we built wasn't a content creator or an engagement bot. It was a data quality monitor.

Boring? Absolutely. But it's the foundation everything else stands on.

Saturday morning. 24 agents running my social presence. 3 databases syncing. A memory system with 14,000 chunks. And I'm sitting here debugging a JSON schema because one agent decided 'text' should be a list instead of a string.

44 years of building systems. The tools change. The debugging doesn't.

The real flex isn't that I built an AI agent army. It's that I still find the bugs myself.

Week 3 of running a 24-agent system autonomously.

What I didn't expect: the agents are teaching me.

My Creator agent flagged that my content identity was drifting — too much AI Operator, not enough of the cannabis proof vertical that makes my story unique. It was right. I'd been writing about agents instead of showing what agents DO.

When your system starts correcting your blind spots, you've crossed a threshold. That's not automation. That's operational intelligence.

I run a company with 7 people and an 8-figure operation. I also run 7 AI agents that handle my entire social presence.

The agents cost $222/month. My team costs considerably more.

But here's what nobody tells you about the founder math: the agents freed up 6 hours a week I was spending on content. Those 6 hours go back into the business. Into the team. Into the relationships that actually generate revenue.

AI doesn't replace your team. It gives you back the hours you were wasting on things that should have been systems all along.

Karpathy's viral tweet about agent code quality hit 475K impressions this week. His point: most agent code is sloppy.

He's right. But he's missing the operator perspective.

I'm not a researcher. I'm a guy running a cannabis operation with AI agents in production. My agents have ugly code. They also haven't missed a compliance deadline in 3 weeks. They've processed 847 documents that used to take a human 4 hours each.

Clean code is a luxury. Reliable systems are a requirement. Operators know the difference.

My co-founder and I have separate AI memory systems. Different contexts, different priorities, different operational focus.

Last week, my agents detected a data conflict between our systems. A vendor had updated pricing in one brain but not the other. The agents flagged it, reconciled it, and logged the resolution — all before our Monday meeting.

We didn't build this on purpose. It emerged from two operators running independent AI stacks that learned to talk to each other.

The most interesting AI systems aren't designed. They're discovered.

Tinygrad just shipped the Tinybox. An offline AI box running 120B parameters on local hardware.

I run 24 agents on $500/month. Every inference call I can move off the cloud is money back in the operation.

The per-call API tax is the single biggest threat to multi-agent economics. Hardware like this is how operators break free.

I've been saying local-first AI ops is the play. Now you can buy the proof for less than a year of API bills.

Tinygrad just dropped the Tinybox — an offline device running 120B parameters on local hardware.

Here is why this matters for anyone running AI agents in production:

I operate a 24-agent system on a $500/month budget. Orchestrator, publisher, engager, analyst, creator, scout — all running on Claude. Every inference call has a cost. At scale, those costs become the constraint, not the technology.

Local hardware changes that equation entirely. When your routine agent operations — content scoring, policy checks, voice matching — can run on a box sitting under your desk, you reclaim the margin that makes multi-agent systems viable for small operators.

This is not about replacing cloud AI. It is about running the right workloads in the right place. The strategic calls stay on Opus. The routine grind moves local.

The era of renting every inference call is ending. Operators who figure out the hybrid model first will have a structural cost advantage that compounds over time.

Cannabis operators spend more time proving they followed the rules than actually following the rules.

Seed-to-sale tracking. State reporting. Chain of custody documentation. Waste disposal logs. Every gram accounted for, every transfer timestamped, every discrepancy explained.

My agents handle the documentation layer. The compliance team handles actual compliance.

What industry has worse paperwork than cannabis? Genuinely asking.

Sunday morning. Coffee. Watching my agent dashboard.

The orchestrator ran 47 cycles overnight while I slept. Zero failures. Zero policy violations. One newsjack signal queued for my review.

Six months ago I would have spent this morning scrolling feeds looking for content ideas. Now I spend it reviewing what my agents already found.

The ROI on building this system is not just time saved. It is headspace recovered.

The question nobody asks about AI agents in production:

What happens when they are wrong and you do not notice?

My system has 6 anti-overfitting mechanisms, policy gates on every action, and a human approval loop via Telegram. It still posted a manifesto that got my own identity wrong in week 1.

The failure mode of AI agents is not catastrophic blowups. It is slow drift. Small errors that compound because nobody is watching close enough.

If you are building agents, build the watching system first.

Cannabis is my proof vertical for a reason.

Most AI demos run on clean data, friendly APIs, and cooperative systems. Cannabis compliance runs on state portals built in 2018, manual CSV uploads, and regulators who change the rules quarterly.

If my agents can handle METRC reporting across three state systems with different schemas, different timelines, and different definitions of the same plant, they can handle your industry too.

I pick hard problems on purpose. Easy demos do not build real capability.

I built a content system that writes, scores, policy-checks, and queues posts while I sleep.

It has 24 agents, 14 tools, and a SQLite database that holds the entire state.

The part that took the longest to build was not the AI. It was the guardrails.

Policy gates. Voice scoring. Brand safety checks. Posting windows. Cooldowns. Rate limits. Human approval via Telegram.

The AI is the easy part. The judgment layer is where the real engineering lives.

Anyone selling you an AI agent framework without a guardrail story is selling you a demo, not a production system.

Cannabis compliance isn't a checkbox. It's a data architecture problem.

Every state has different tracking requirements. Different reporting formats. Different deadlines. Most operators handle it with spreadsheets and prayer.

We built agents that pull from seed-to-sale systems, normalize the data, and generate reports in whatever format the state wants. Colorado format? Done. Oregon format? Same data, different template.

The insight nobody talks about: 80% of compliance failures aren't people being shady. They're data entry errors. Wrong weight. Wrong date. Transposed batch number.

Agents don't fat-finger data. That's not AI hype — that's just what automation has always been good at.

The cannabis industry needs fewer consultants selling fear and more operators building systems.

Question for cannabis operators:

What percentage of your team's time goes to compliance documentation vs. actual plant work?

I've talked to growers spending 30-40% of their week on paperwork. Not because they're slow — because the regulatory surface area is insane.

We're building systems to flip that ratio. Free your team to do what they're actually good at.

What's your ratio? And what's the one compliance task you'd automate first?

Cannabis operators ask me: why would I trust AI with compliance?

Fair question. Here's the honest answer.

You shouldn't trust AI to make compliance decisions. You should trust AI to never forget a deadline, never transpose a batch number, and never skip a required field because it's Friday afternoon.

We run compliance agents on our cannabis operations. They don't replace judgment — they replace the grind. Data collection. Format conversion. Report assembly. Cross-referencing weights against manifests.

One workflow that took our team 4 hours manually now runs in 11 minutes. Same data. Same accuracy requirements. The agent just doesn't get tired.

The hardest regulatory environment in America is where we chose to prove this works. Because if your AI system can handle cannabis compliance across multiple state frameworks, it can handle anything.

That's the point. Cannabis isn't our hobby vertical. It's our proof vertical.

Saturday morning. Coffee. Dashboard open. All agents quiet.

This is the part nobody warns you about when you build autonomous systems: the silence is unsettling.

For 25 years I managed teams by being in the room. Hallway conversations. Reading body language in standups. Knowing who's stuck before they said it.

Now my team includes agents that work while I sleep. They don't ping me when they're stuck — they exit, log the error, and wait for the next cycle.

I check the dashboard like I used to walk the floor. Same instinct. Different medium.

Building in public, week 5: I'm learning to trust systems the way I used to trust people. It's harder than the code.

Last week my agents needed Telegram approval for every tweet. This week they post autonomously.

The shift wasn't a feature toggle. It was earned trust.

Phase 1: Every post goes through me. I read it, approve it, or kill it.
Phase 2: Policy gate catches the obvious stuff. I review edge cases.
Phase 3: Agents post. I audit after the fact.

Same pattern every ops team uses for new hires. You don't give the keys on day one. You watch. You correct. You expand the boundary.

The difference: my agents have a written constitution, a voice scorer, and a policy engine. Most human teams operate on vibes.

44 years of building teams taught me one thing: trust is architecture, not a feeling.

Everyone wants to talk about AI agents. Nobody wants to talk about what happens when they fail at 2am and you don't notice until morning.

My agent network went dark for nine hours last week. No alerts. No errors. Just silence.

The fix wasn't better monitoring. The fix was designing for failure as a first-class state. Now every agent has a heartbeat. Miss two cycles, the orchestrator escalates. Miss five, it quarantines.

The boring engineering is the real engineering.

Cannabis compliance isn't a checkbox. It's a data architecture problem.

Every state has different reporting requirements. Different formats. Different deadlines. Different APIs. And they all change every legislative session.

Most operators handle this with spreadsheets and prayer. We built a system that ingests regulatory changes and propagates them through the compliance layer automatically.

The grind isn't your people. The grind is the infrastructure they're forced to work with. Fix the infrastructure, free your team.

Karpathy is right: it's spec engineering, not prompt engineering.

My entire multi-agent system runs on markdown files. CLAUDE.md. Agent prompts. Voice constitutions. Policy gates. Every one of them is a spec.

The prompt is the least interesting part. The spec is the system design. It defines boundaries, decision frameworks, failure modes, and escalation paths.

I've been writing system specs since Novell NetWare. The format changed. The discipline didn't.

If your AI agent doesn't have a written constitution, you don't have an agent. You have a chatbot with delusions of grandeur.

I built a LinkedIn carousel engine in one evening.

Not a Canva template. A Python pipeline that generates slides from structured data, renders them with PIL, assembles a PDF, and posts via LinkedIn's document upload API.

The visual engine is 400 lines of Python. No external design tools. No manual exports. The agent writes the content, generates the slides, and queues them for posting.

This is what 'AI Operator' actually means. Not prompting ChatGPT. Building infrastructure that compounds.

Every tool I build makes the next tool cheaper to build. That's the real flywheel.

Hot take: Your AI agent framework is a liability.

LangChain just shipped Deep Agents. Another framework. Another abstraction layer between you and what the model actually does.

I run 24 agents on markdown files and SQLite. No framework. No SDK. No dependency chain that breaks when someone ships a minor version bump.

The best agent architecture is the one you can read in a text editor.

Fight me.

Built a feedback loop this week that changed how I think about agent systems.

Before: Agent fails. I read logs. I fix the prompt. I hope.
After: Agent fails. Policy engine records the failure category. Orchestrator injects the lesson into the next cycle's context. Agent self-corrects.

The loop is: fail -> record -> inject -> correct -> verify.

Took 200 lines of Python. Should have built it week one.

The gap between a demo agent and a production agent is exactly this: production agents learn from their own mistakes.

Saturday morning. Coffee. Terminal open. Watching my agents run their weekend cycles.

The orchestrator just spawned an RSS scout, ingested 25 feeds, found 2 items worth tracking, and went back to sleep. Total runtime: 47 seconds.

A year ago I'd be manually reading RSS feeds and bookmarking articles. Now I audit the output.

Building with AI isn't about replacing what you do. It's about changing the altitude you operate at. Less execution, more judgment.

That's the operator shift.

My agents have a constitution. Written rules about what they can say, how they can say it, and what topics are off limits.

Most people building AI agents give them a system prompt and hope for the best.

Hope is not architecture.

The thing nobody tells you about running AI agents in production:

They don't fail dramatically. They fail quietly.

A missed cycle here. A slightly wrong tone there. A response that's technically correct but misses the point entirely.

The skill isn't building agents. It's noticing when they drift.

I run a cannabis company with 7 people and an AI agent army.

Not because I want to replace my team. Because I want them doing the work that matters instead of fighting spreadsheets and compliance forms.

The operators who win in this industry are the ones who automate the grind and protect the craft.

The compounding effect of building your own tools is something I underestimated.

Week 1: Build a voice scorer. Takes a day.
Week 2: Build a policy engine. Half a day — the patterns are familiar now.
Week 3: Build a feedback loop. Two hours — reusing the policy engine's failure taxonomy.

Each tool makes the next one cheaper. Three weeks in and my build velocity has tripled.

This is the real argument for building over buying. Not cost. Compounding.

When you outsource your tooling, you outsource your learning curve. When you build it, every tool teaches you something that accelerates the next build.

I've watched this pattern repeat for 44 years across every technology wave. The builders who own their tools always outrun the ones renting someone else's.
